intro: July 2011. You are Reed Hastings. Netflix is worth $16B and you just beat Blockbuster to death. Now you want to split DVDs from streaming — and you're about to light 800,000 subscribers on fire to do it.

realOutcome.summary: Netflix's 2011 was a masterclass in being strategically right and tactically catastrophic — then recovering. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: DVD-plus-streaming costs $9.99. Streaming is the future but DVDs still print money. You want to unbundle them — which means customers keeping both will pay $15.98, a 60% price hike overnight.

choices.text: Unbundle and raise. Rip the band-aid off now.
choices.reveal: Hastings did it and lost 800,000 subscribers in one quarter while the stock fell from $300 to $65. But separating streaming's economics from DVDs is what funded the next decade. Right call, brutal year — this one hurts before it pays.

choices.text: Keep the bundle. Never anger the customer base.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Peace in 2011, and streaming stays chained to a declining DVD business's cost structure. You've postponed the surgery until the patient is weaker.

choices.text: Grandfather existing users, raise prices for new ones only.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Two customer classes, one support nightmare, and the revenue math doesn't move for three years. Kindness as strategy is still indecision.

decisions.scenario: September 2011. Subscribers are furious about the hike. Your response plan: apologize, then announce the DVD business will be spun into a separate site called 'Qwikster' — separate queue, separate billing, separate ratings.

choices.text: Launch Qwikster. Commit to the separation completely.
choices.reveal: Hastings announced it in a rambling apology video. Customers who'd swallowed the price hike now faced two websites and two bills. Even the @Qwikster Twitter handle belonged to someone else. It became a business-school case study in unforced errors.

choices.text: Just apologize for the price shock. No new announcements.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: An apology that doesn't introduce a second problem. The subscriber bleed slows months earlier, and 'Qwikster' never enters the business-school curriculum.

choices.text: Double down publicly: 'the price is the price.'
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Defiance reads as contempt while your stock is down 60%. You're strategically right and you still need customers to like you.

decisions.scenario: Three weeks post-announcement. Qwikster is a national joke, the stock is down 75% from July, and your own executives are telling you it's a mistake. Reversing means admitting the CEO was wrong, publicly, mid-crisis.

choices.text: Kill Qwikster. One sentence, no spin: we were wrong.
choices.reveal: Hastings reversed in three weeks flat — 'It is clear that for many of our members, two websites would make things more difficult.' The speed of the reversal, not the original call, is what saved the company's credibility.

choices.text: Ride it out. Reversing looks weak.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Qwikster limps along for 18 months, splitting engineering focus during the exact window Amazon and Hulu are arming up. Ego is expensive at scale.

choices.text: Rebrand it. The idea's fine, the name's the problem.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: 'DVD.com by Netflix' has the same two logins and two bills. Customers weren't mocking the name; they were mocking the idea.

decisions.scenario: 2012. You're wounded — stock at $8B, press writing obituaries. International expansion (Latin America, UK, Nordics) is burning hundreds of millions a year with no profit in sight. The board wants to know if now is really the time.

choices.text: Keep burning. Plant the flag in every market before rivals do.
choices.reveal: Netflix kept expanding straight through the crisis — international went from money pit to the majority of all subscribers. The land grab only worked because it happened while everyone else was hesitating.

choices.text: Pause international. Fix the home market first.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Prudent, defensible, and fatal. Amazon Prime Video launches into every market you postponed, and 'the Netflix of Europe' gets built by someone else.

choices.text: License your platform to foreign broadcasters instead.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Quick cash, and you've trained a dozen national champions to run streaming services that will never need you again.

decisions.scenario: Late 2012. A political drama pitch called 'House of Cards' is on the table — but the deal requires committing $100M for two full seasons, sight unseen, no pilot. HBO passed on those terms. You'd be a DVD company outbidding Hollywood.

choices.text: Commit the $100M. Two seasons, no pilot, full creative freedom.
choices.reveal: House of Cards premiered February 2013, all episodes at once — inventing binge culture and proving Netflix could out-prestige HBO. The stock rose ~300% in 2013. Original content became the moat.

choices.text: Offer a standard pilot deal like everyone else.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Fincher and Spacey take the two-season guarantee elsewhere. The no-pilot commitment WAS the pitch — matching Hollywood's process gets you Hollywood's rejects.

choices.text: Skip originals. Spend the $100M licensing proven hits.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You rent your entire catalog from studios that are all quietly building their own streaming services. In five years they take the shows back and you're a pipe.

bands.line: You survived your own worst year and bought the next decade. Legend episode: WON.
Alive, but the moat never got dug. Amazon is very interested in acquiring you.
Qwikster forever. Blockbuster's ghost is laughing. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Netflix 40x'd after this fork. Today's agents flagged a company mid-pivot at a similar crossroads →
